section.title: The Sermon on the Mount: Prayer and Treasure
section.sectionRationale: The Sermon turns from outward conduct to the hidden life before God. Jesus warns against practicing giving, prayer, and fasting to be seen by others, gives the model prayer, and then calls His people to a single-hearted devotion that stores treasure in heaven, serves God rather than money, and trusts the Father instead of being consumed by worry.
section.thesis: True righteousness is lived before the Father who sees in secret, not staged for human applause, and it flows from a heart set on God alone, so that His people pray to their Father, treasure heaven above earth, serve God rather than money, and are freed from anxiety to seek first His kingdom.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: giving in secret; prayer without hypocrisy; the model prayer; forgiveness; secret fasting; treasure in heaven and undivided service; and freedom from worry through trust in the Father.

units.label: Giving in secret
units.note: your Father who sees in secret

verses.text: “Be careful not to perform your righteous acts before men to be seen by them. If you do, you will have no reward from your Father in heaven.

So when you give to the needy, do not sound a trumpet before you, as the {{hypokrites|hypocrites}} do in the synagogues and on the streets, to be honored by men. Truly I tell you, they already have their full reward.
But when you give to the needy, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing,
so that your giving may be in secret. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.

units.label: How to pray
units.note: your Father knows what you need

verses.text: And when you pray, do not be like the hypocrites. For they love to pray standing in the synagogues and on the street corners to be seen by men. Truly I tell you, they already have their full reward.
But when you pray, go into your inner room, shut your door, and pray to your Father, who is unseen. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.
And when you pray, do not babble on like pagans, for they think that by their many words they will be heard.
Do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need before you ask Him.
So then, this is how you should pray: ‘Our Father in heaven, hallowed be Your name.
Your kingdom come, Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven.
Give us this day our {{epiousios|daily}} bread.
And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors.
And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one.’
For if you forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.
But if you do not forgive men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive yours.
When you fast, do not be somber like the hypocrites, for they disfigure their faces to show men they are fasting. Truly I tell you, they already have their full reward.
But when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face,
so that your fasting will not be obvious to men, but only to your Father, who is unseen. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.

units.label: Treasure and the single eye
units.note: you cannot serve God and money

verses.text: Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal.
But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where moth and rust do not destroy, and where thieves do not break in and steal.
For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.
The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eyes are good, your whole body will be full of light.
But if your eyes are bad, your whole body will be full of darkness. If then the light within you is darkness, how great is that darkness!
No one can serve two masters: Either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and {{mamonas|money}}.
Therefore I tell you, do not {{merimnao|worry}} about your life, what you will eat or drink; or about your body, what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothes?
Look at the birds of the air: They do not sow or reap or gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not much more valuable than they?
Who of you by worrying can add a single hour to his life?
And why do you worry about clothes? Consider how the lilies of the field grow: They do not labor or spin.
Yet I tell you that not even Solomon in all his glory was adorned like one of these.
If that is how God clothes the grass of the field, which is here today and tomorrow is thrown into the furnace, will He not much more clothe you, O you of little faith?
Therefore do not worry, saying, ‘What shall we eat?’ or ‘What shall we drink?’ or ‘What shall we wear?’
For the Gentiles strive after all these things, and your heavenly Father knows that you need them.
But seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added unto you.
Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Today has enough trouble of its own.

hypokrites.gloss: Hypocrite, originally an actor who plays a part behind a mask. Jesus uses it for those who perform their religion for an audience of people rather than God; they win human applause and forfeit the Father's reward.

epiousios.gloss: A rare word rendered daily, likely meaning the bread needed for the coming day. It teaches dependence on the Father for each day's provision, echoing Israel's daily manna in the wilderness.

mamonas.gloss: Mammon, wealth or money personified as a rival master. Jesus presents money as a would-be lord that competes with God for the heart's allegiance; one cannot be a slave to both.

merimnao.gloss: To be anxious, worried, pulled apart by care. Jesus repeatedly forbids this corroding worry over food and clothing, calling His people instead to trust the Father's provision and seek first His kingdom.

basic.context: Jesus moves from how we treat others to the hidden life of devotion. He warns that giving, praying, and fasting done to impress people get only the reward of being noticed. Instead, do them before the Father who sees in secret. He gives the model prayer, teaches that we must forgive to be forgiven, and then calls for a single-hearted life: store treasure in heaven, serve God not money, and stop worrying, because the Father feeds the birds and clothes the lilies.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the first half as a critique of religion performed for an audience, with the refrain your Father who sees in secret. Read the Lord's Prayer as a pattern to shape all our praying, not just words to recite. The second half exposes the heart's true master: what we treasure, whom we serve, and what we worry about all reveal where our trust really lies.
basic.originalLanguages: The hypocrites are play-actors, performing religion for applause. In the prayer we ask for daily bread, the provision needed for each coming day, like manna. Jesus names money as a rival master, mammon, that competes with God for our hearts. And He forbids the corroding worry that pulls us apart, calling us to trust the Father instead.
basic.typology: The daily bread echoes the manna God gave Israel each morning in the wilderness, teaching daily dependence. The Father who clothes the lilies and feeds the birds is the Creator of Genesis, still sustaining His world. And the treasure in heaven points to the inheritance kept for God's people that no thief can touch.
basic.christ: Jesus teaches us to call God our Father, a intimacy grounded in His own Sonship, into which He brings us. He is the one whose whole life was lived before the Father, not for human applause. He is the treasure worth more than all the world, and the one who models perfect trust in the Father's care, even to the cross.
basic.god: The Father is the great theme: He sees in secret and rewards, He knows our needs before we ask, He feeds the birds and clothes the grass, and He is worthy of undivided service. He is a Father to be trusted, not a distant power to be manipulated with many words or impressed with public displays.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 16 gives the daily manna behind daily bread. Luke 11 has a parallel form of the Lord's Prayer. 1 Timothy 6 warns that the love of money is a root of evil. And 1 Peter 5 urges us to cast our anxiety on God because He cares.
basic.oneStory: The King calls His people to live the whole of life before the face of their Father. Religion becomes real when it is done for God's eyes, not the crowd's; prayer becomes intimate when addressed to a Father who already knows our needs; and life becomes free when the heart treasures God above money and trusts Him for daily bread. All of it flows from knowing God as Father, the relationship Jesus came to open.

context.literary: This central section of the Sermon is carefully structured. Verses 1 to 18 treat the three classic acts of Jewish piety, giving, prayer, and fasting, each with the same pattern: not like the hypocrites, but in secret before the Father. The Lord's Prayer sits at the heart. Verses 19 to 34 then address the heart's treasure, master, and worries, all turning on trust in the Father.
context.historical: Almsgiving, prayer, and fasting were the pillars of Jewish devotion. Public prayer at fixed hours, visible fasting, and conspicuous giving offered real temptations to showmanship. Mammon was an Aramaic term for wealth. The images, birds, lilies, grass thrown in the oven, moth and rust, are drawn from everyday Galilean life and the fragility of earthly goods.
context.canonical: The Lord's Prayer distills the Old Testament hope, God's name hallowed, His kingdom come, His will done, into a pattern for the church's prayer, and Luke 11 preserves a parallel. The teaching on money and worry develops the wisdom tradition's warnings about riches and trust, and the whole section deepens the Bible's call to love God with an undivided heart.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call God's people to a sincere devotion lived before the Father who sees in secret rather than staged for human praise, to teach them how to pray, and to free them for single-hearted trust that treasures heaven, serves God alone, and refuses anxious worry.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This section is largely prescriptive, teaching how disciples are to give, pray, fast, handle money, and face anxiety. The promises attached, the Father's reward and provision, are assurances for those who trust and obey, not mechanical guarantees; the commands assume hearts already turned to God by grace.

debates.question: What does daily bread mean in the Lord's Prayer?

views.label: The provision needed for each day
views.case: The rare word most naturally means the bread necessary for the coming day, teaching daily dependence on the Father, echoing the manna Israel gathered each morning.

views.label: A richer, forward-looking bread
views.case: Some see an echo of the bread of the age to come or the sustaining grace of God's kingdom, so that the petition reaches beyond mere food to God's full provision for His people.

debates.bearing: Either way the petition teaches humble, daily reliance on the Father for all we need, body and soul, rather than anxious self-sufficiency.

debates.question: Does our forgiving others earn God's forgiveness?

views.label: Forgiving is the evidence, not the cause, of being forgiven
views.case: Those truly forgiven by God become forgiving; a persistently unforgiving heart shows it has never grasped grace, so Jesus warns that such a person is not in fact reconciled to God.

views.label: A real, covenantal condition of ongoing fellowship
views.case: Jesus states a genuine link: the Father withholds forgiveness from the unforgiving, not because grace is earned, but because refusing to forgive others is incompatible with living under His mercy.

debates.bearing: Both agree that a settled refusal to forgive cannot coexist with receiving God's forgiveness; grace received always overflows in grace given.

study.originalLanguages: The hypokrites are actors in masks, performing devotion for a human audience and so losing the Father's reward; the antidote is the repeated your Father who sees in secret. In the model prayer we ask for epiousios bread, the provision for each coming day, a call to daily trust like Israel's manna. Jesus then unmasks the heart's rival lord, mamonas, money that demands the allegiance owed to God, warning that no one can serve two masters. And He forbids merimnao, the anxious worry that tears us apart, pointing to a Father who feeds birds and clothes lilies and calling us to seek first His kingdom.

typology.fulfillment: The petition echoes the daily manna of Exodus 16, teaching God's people to depend on the Father for each day's provision.

typology.type: The Father who clothes and feeds His creatures
typology.fulfillment: The Creator of Genesis who sustains all life is the Father who will surely provide for His children, Psalm 145:15-16.
The imperishable inheritance kept for God's people, secured in Christ and beyond the reach of moth, rust, and thief, 1 Peter 1:4.

study.christ: At the center of this teaching is the astonishing invitation to call God our Father, an intimacy that flows from Jesus' own eternal Sonship and the relationship He came to share with us. He is the one who lived every moment before the Father's eyes and never for the applause of the crowd, who fasted and prayed in secret, and who trusted His Father's care completely, even when it led to the cross. He is Himself the treasure surpassing all earthly wealth, the one for whom it is worth losing everything. In teaching us to pray and to trust, He is drawing us into His own life with the Father.
study.god: The Father dominates this chapter. He sees what is done in secret and rewards it; He knows our needs before we ask; He feeds the birds, clothes the lilies, and much more will care for His children; and He is worthy of an undivided heart that will not share His throne with money. This is not a God to be manipulated by long prayers or impressed by public displays, but a Father to be trusted. To know Him rightly is to be set free from performing for people and from being consumed by worry, and to rest in His fatherly provision while seeking first His kingdom.

crossReferences.note: God gives Israel bread from heaven day by day, the pattern behind give us this day our daily bread.
A parallel form of the Lord's Prayer given in response to the disciples' request to be taught to pray.
The love of money as a root of all kinds of evil, illustrating the choice between God and mammon.
Cast all your anxiety on Him because He cares for you, the answer to worry Jesus commands.

study.oneStory: Under the King's reign the whole of life is lived before the Father's face. Religion is freed from the theater of human applause and offered instead to the God who sees in secret; prayer becomes the intimate speech of children to a Father who already knows their needs; and the heart is set free from the tyranny of money and worry to treasure God above all. Every command flows from one great gift Jesus came to give, the right to know God as Father, and it moves toward the day when His kingdom fully comes and His will is done on earth as in heaven.

quiz.q: What is Jesus' main warning about giving, prayer, and fasting?

options.text: Do not do them to be seen by others, but before the Father who sees in secret.
options.note: Verses 1-18 repeat this contrast between human applause and the Father's reward.

options.text: Do them privately so that no one is ever helped by your example.
options.note: The aim is sincerity before God, not hiding all good works; Jesus elsewhere says let your light shine. This overstates secrecy.

options.text: Avoid them altogether to escape the temptation of pride.
options.note: Jesus assumes His people will give, pray, and fast; the issue is the audience and motive, not abstaining.

options.text: Perform them publicly to encourage others to do the same.
options.note: The opposite of His warning; doing them to be seen forfeits the Father's reward.

quiz.q: How does Jesus teach His disciples to pray?

options.text: To their Father who knows their needs, using the pattern of the Lord's Prayer.
options.note: Verses 6-13 direct prayer to the Father and give the model prayer.

options.text: With many repeated words to be sure God hears them.
options.note: Jesus rejects this pagan babbling; He wants trusting, simple prayer to the Father who already knows our needs.

options.text: Only in public gatherings at the synagogue.
options.note: He commends private prayer to the Father, not public display, as the primary posture.

options.text: Silently, since God does not want us to ask for anything.
options.note: Jesus tells us to ask, giving a whole prayer of petitions; God invites our requests.

quiz.q: Why should disciples store up treasures in heaven rather than on earth?

options.text: Earthly treasure perishes, and where your treasure is, your heart will be also.
options.note: Verses 19-21 tie the location of treasure to the direction of the heart.

options.text: Because owning any earthly possessions is sinful.
options.note: The issue is where the heart's treasure lies, not that possessions themselves are sinful.

options.text: Because heaven rewards those who first grow rich on earth.
options.note: Jesus contrasts the two treasures; he does not commend first storing up earthly wealth.

options.text: Because God has no interest in how we use money.
options.note: The whole passage shows God cares deeply how we treasure and use money.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by you cannot serve both God and money?

options.text: Money is a rival master; the heart cannot give undivided devotion to both it and God.
options.note: Verse 24 presents money as a competing lord demanding the allegiance owed to God.

options.text: It is impossible to be both wealthy and a believer.
options.note: The point is undivided allegiance, not that wealth and faith cannot coexist; many faithful people had means.

options.text: We should refuse to earn or handle money at all.
options.note: Jesus addresses the heart's master, not the mere handling of money.

options.text: God and money can be served equally with balance.
options.note: Jesus explicitly denies this; no one can serve two masters.

quiz.q: What is the remedy Jesus gives for anxiety?

options.text: Trust the Father who feeds the birds and clothes the lilies, and seek first His kingdom.
options.note: Verses 25-34 ground freedom from worry in the Father's care and the priority of His kingdom.

options.text: Work harder so you never lack food or clothing.
options.note: Jesus does not commend anxious striving; He points to trust in the Father, not self-reliance.

options.text: Ignore all practical needs as unspiritual.
options.note: He acknowledges real needs, the Father knows you need them, but calls for trust, not neglect.

options.text: Accept that God is indifferent to your daily needs.
options.note: The opposite; the Father knows and provides, so worry is unwarranted.

questions: Jesus says the Father sees in secret. How would living for an audience of One, rather than for people's approval, change the way you serve, give, or pray?
The Lord's Prayer begins with God's name, kingdom, and will before our needs. How might reordering your prayers this way reshape what you ask for?
Jesus links being forgiven with forgiving others. Is there someone you are refusing to forgive, and how does the mercy you have received in Christ move you toward them?
Where your treasure is, your heart will be also. What does the way you spend your money and time reveal about what you truly treasure?
Jesus calls anxious disciples you of little faith, then points to a caring Father. What worry is dominating you right now, and what would it look like to seek first the kingdom and trust Him with it?

translationNotes.note: Daily renders the rare word epiousios, most likely meaning the bread needed for the coming day, a call to daily dependence on the Father.
Deliver us from the evil one may also read from evil; some later manuscripts add the doxology, For Yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory, forever. Amen, noted in the BSB footnote.
Money renders mammon, an Aramaic term for wealth, here pictured as a rival master competing with God for the heart.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 6:1-34. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; poem, line-break, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
